description_en: An asset or object bought or obtained, typically by a library or museum.
example: The company announced the acquisition of its main competitor.

description_en: The state of being bankrupt.
example: The retail chain filed for bankruptcy after years of declining sales.
note: Legal status of a person or entity that cannot repay debts.

description_en: Wealth in the form of money or other assets owned by a person or organization.
example: The startup raised $1 million in seed capital.
note: Essential for starting or expanding a business.

description_en: A sum of money paid regularly by a company to its shareholders out of its profits.
example: The company decided to increase its annual dividend.

description_en: The value of the shares issued by a company.
example: He owns 20% equity in the family business.

description_en: Relating to government revenue, especially taxes.
example: The government's fiscal policy aims to reduce the national deficit.

description_en: The profit a company makes after deducting the costs associated with making and selling its products.
example: Our gross profit margin improved this quarter.

description_en: A general increase in prices and fall in the purchasing value of money.
example: Central banks use interest rates to control inflation.

description_en: A commercial enterprise undertaken jointly by two or more parties which otherwise retain their distinct identities.
example: They formed a joint venture to build a new factory in Asia.

description_en: The availability of liquid assets to a market or company.
example: Maintaining high liquidity is important for meeting short-term obligations.
note: Ease of converting assets into cash.

description_en: A combination of two things, especially companies, into one.
example: The merger of the two banks was approved by regulators.

description_en: The actual profit after working expenses not included in the calculation of gross profit have been paid.
example: Our net profit doubled compared to last year.

description_en: Business expenses, such as rent, that are not directly attributed to any specific business activity.
example: We are trying to reduce our monthly overhead costs.

description_en: A range of investments held by a person or organization.
example: Diversifying your portfolio can help manage investment risk.

description_en: Income, especially when of a company or organization and of a substantial nature.
example: The company's annual revenue reached $5 billion.
note: Total amount of money brought in.

description_en: An owner of shares in a company.
example: The company's performance has pleased the shareholders.

description_en: A company controlled by a holding company.
example: Google is a subsidiary of Alphabet Inc.
note: The controlling company is the parent.

description_en: An estimation of something's worth.
example: The company's market valuation dropped after the news.

description_en: Capital invested in a project in which there is a substantial element of risk, typically a new or expanding business.
example: The startup is looking for venture capital to scale its operations.

description_en: The selling of goods in large quantities to be retailed by others.
example: We buy our supplies at wholesale prices.

description_en: An official inspection of an individual's or organization's accounts.
example: The company underwent an independent audit last month.

description_en: A standard or point of reference against which things may be compared or assessed.
example: Our performance is measured against industry benchmarks.
note: Used for comparison and goal setting.

description_en: A raw material or primary agricultural product that can be bought and sold.
example: Oil and gold are two of the most traded commodities.

description_en: Reduction of the general level of prices in an economy.
example: The country experienced a period of deflation in the 1990s.

description_en: The process of a business enlarging or varying its range of products or field of operation.
example: Diversification is a strategy to reduce business risk.
note: Common in investment and business growth.

description_en: A person who organizes and operates a business or businesses, taking on greater than normal financial risks.
example: She is a successful tech entrepreneur.
note: Innovation and risk-taking are key traits.

description_en: An authorization granted by a government or company to an individual or group enabling them to carry out specified commercial activities.
example: McDonald's is one of the most famous franchises in the world.

description_en: Protect oneself against loss on (a bet or investment) by making balancing or compensating transactions.
example: The firm uses currency hedging to protect against exchange rate fluctuations.

meaning: sự mất khả năng thanh toán
description_en: The state of being unable to pay one's debts.
example: The company was forced into insolvency due to lack of cash.

description_en: The use of credit or borrowed funds to improve one's speculative capacity and increase the rate of return.
example: The firm used high leverage to finance its expansion.
note: Increases both potential profit and risk.

description_en: The exclusive possession or control of the supply or trade in a commodity or service.
example: Government laws prevent the creation of monopolies to ensure fair competition.
note: Reduces choices and can inflate prices.

description_en: A specialized segment of the market for a particular kind of product or service.
example: They found a profitable niche in organic baby food.

description_en: Obtain goods or a service from an outside or foreign supplier, especially in place of an internal source.
example: Many companies are outsourcing their customer support to save costs.

description_en: A business or firm owned and run by two or more partners.
example: The two consultants formed a partnership last year.

description_en: A fixed share of something that a person or group is entitled to receive or is bound to contribute.
example: The government imposed an import quota on luxury cars.
note: Used in trade and sales targets.

description_en: A period of temporary economic decline during which trade and industrial activity are reduced.
example: The global economy was hit hard by the 2008 recession.
note: Marked by two consecutive quarters of negative growth.

description_en: A person with an interest or concern in something, especially a business.
example: The company holds regular meetings with all its stakeholders.

description_en: A tax or duty to be paid on a particular class of imports or exports.
example: The new trade agreement will remove tariffs on agricultural products.

description_en: Sign and accept liability under (an insurance policy), thus guaranteeing payment in case loss or damage occurs.
example: Investment banks handle the underwriting of initial public offerings (IPOs).
note: Assessing and taking on financial risk.

description_en: Liability to change rapidly and unpredictably, especially for the worse.
example: The stock market volatility has increased recently.
note: Measurement of risk in financial markets.

description_en: A written guarantee, issued to the purchaser of an article by its manufacturer, promising to repair or replace it if necessary.
example: The laptop comes with a two-year manufacturer's warranty.

description_en: The income return on an investment, usually expressed as an annual percentage.
example: High-yield bonds offer better returns but carry more risk.

description_en: A person who buys and sells goods or assets for others.
example: I contacted a stock broker to help manage my investments.

description_en: A reduction in the value of an asset over time, due in particular to wear and tear.
example: The car's depreciation was much faster than I expected.
note: Accounting method to distribute asset cost.

description_en: The action of spending funds.
example: Government expenditure on education has increased this year.

description_en: A thing that motivates or encourages someone to do something.
example: The company offers tax incentives for new investors.

description_en: The difference between the seller's cost for acquiring a product and the selling price.
example: Retailers often operate on thin profit margins.

description_en: A printed booklet advertising a school or university or a formal document that provides details about an investment offering.
example: Investors should read the prospectus carefully before buying shares.

description_en: The possession of assets in excess of liabilities; ability to pay one's debts.
example: The central bank tests the solvency of commercial banks regularly.

description_en: An instance of buying or selling something.
example: Online banking allows you to track all your transactions.
note: Found in both retail and finance.
